data.title: Policy Risks A Distant Second To The Real Economy
data.description:
By Russ Koesterich, CFA, JD

In this article, Russ Koesterich discusses the resilience in the U.S. economy and why he believes stocks will continue their climb higher through year-end.

The list of policy-related risks is long, varied and growing, including lingering tariff uncertainty, a government shutdown with the potential to exacerbate an already soft labor market, and rising geopolitical tension in Europe. And of course, these risks are occurring against the backdrop of a stock market that is not exactly cheap.

The collective response of equity investors? Bid stocks to record highs. While more bearish investors can rightly point to high valuations and unpriced tail risks, a rising market is not as irrational as it might seem.

Three factors support investors downplaying policy risks. The economy and earnings are solid; rates are moving lower amidst a collapse of volatility; investors have generally been paid to fade geopolitical and policy risks.

Returning to Trend

One of the main reasons investors are looking past the tariffs and government shutdown is the ongoing resilience in the U.S. economy. Last spring, at the peak of tariff uncertainty, economic expectations collapsed. At that time, the average expected 2025 GDP forecast of a Bloomberg survey of economists fell from 2.3% in February to 1.4% in May.

It has subsequently rebounded to 1.8%, a level broadly consistent with trend growth. And as economic expectations have risen, so have earnings expectations. After plunging in the first half of the year, analysts now expect better than 12% growth for S&P 500 earnings over next year.

Calmer Waters

Recently, investors have derived further support from the bond market. 10-year bond yields are approximately 0.50% below the spring peak, with the decrease mostly a function of lower real rates, indicating easier financial conditions.

Not only are yields lower, but markets are calmer. The MOVE Index, which measures volatility in the Treasury market, is roughly 50% below its spring peak and at the lowest level since early 2022 (see Chart 1).

U.S Treasury Volatility

Source: LSEG Datastream, Merrill Lynch and BlackRock Investment Institute. Oct 01, 2025

Note: MOVE Index is a measure of implied volatility on 1-month U.S. Treasury options.

While headlines may rattle markets, during the past several decades, investors have been conditioned to fade geopolitical risk, from North Korean missile launches to government shutdowns. In most instances, buying any dip proved a profitable strategy. It is also helpful that few of the economic transmission mechanisms that can accompany a potential crisis, such as higher oil prices, are currently an issue.

What the market would not look through

None of which is to suggest that investors will or should ignore any shock. What events would have a higher likelihood of derailing the market rally? Three potential flags to watch: geopolitical tension leading to higher oil prices, tariffs causing a more meaningful spike in inflation and/or a prolonged government shutdown that materially weakens the labor market.

So far, my base case is oil prices stay low, inflation while still elevated is manageable and the shutdown is unlikely to derail the economy. Until proven otherwise, the path of least resistance for stocks in Q4 is higher.

© 2025 BlackRock, Inc. or its affiliates. All rights reserved.

Russ Koesterich, CFA, is a Portfolio Manager for BlackRock's Global Allocation Fund and Lead Portfolio Manager for BlackRock’s Global Allocation (GA) Selects Model Portfolios and is a regular contributor to Market Insights.

A significant portion of the aggregate world gold holdings is owned by governments, central banks and related institutions. One or more of these institutions could sell in amounts large enough to cause a decline in world gold prices. Should there be an increase in the level of hedge activity of gold producing companies, it could cause a decline in world gold prices. Should the speculative community take a negative view towards gold, it could cause a decline in world gold prices.

data.title: Hafnia: Bigger Payouts If Clean Tanker Rates Pop
data.description:
Last week, I covered BW LPG (BWLP), which falls under the BW Group, which in turn has stakes in several energy infrastructure businesses. Notably, these businesses, like Hafnia (NYSE:HAFN), where BW Group has a stake of about 44%, or DHT Holdings (DHT), where they own just under 16%, tend to have dividend policies structured in a way that leads to significant distributions back to BW Group.

Hafnia, in particular, which owns and manages one of the largest product tanker fleets in the world with a total of 198 vessels, is obviously significantly influenced by BW Group. In fact, I believe that BW Group holds a substantial enough stake in Hafnia to, apart from wanting to maximize distributions, also want to preserve and grow the equity value.

We can see that this premise goes hand in hand with Hafnia's dividend policy, which ensures that the company remains well capitalized without risking overdistributing at the expense of the balance sheet's health (not uncommon in shipping).

The company allocates a portion of the quarterly net profit (adjusted for one-offs) that goes up as leverage goes down, and vice versa. As you can see in the slide below, Hafnia pays 50% of net income if net LTV > 40%, 60% at ≤ 40%, 80% at ≤ 30%, and 90% at ≤ 20%, and this has led to a stable net LTV ratio despite the cyclical tanker rates and thus, cyclical profits.

Dividends Have Been Down

Now, despite Hafnia maintaining a low leverage of 24.1% at the end of Q2, which falls into the 80% payout bracket, the actual dividend for the period was just $0.121. Last year, it was over 3x that, at $0.4049. Of course, it's natural to see these swings because the industry is highly cyclical, which can lead to great swings in rates and, thus, even greater swings in earnings as margins expand and contract very easily. Indeed, TCE revenue fell 45% to $231.2 million in Q2, so net income tanked an even greater 71% to $75.3 million.

The reason behind this YoY decline was that both the demand and supply sides of the market were quite weak. Demand-wise, Middle East-to-Asia LR2 runs that were booming last year were sitting closer to the WS 120s through June–July, and the Atlantic MR voyage from Europe to the U.S. was soft enough that per-day earnings sadly sank into the single-digit thousands at times.

On the supply side, availability didn’t tighten to help offset the softer demand. The product tanker order book is at around 20% of the fleet, so there were plenty of ships coming online and competing for cargo. Even with some LR2s drifting into crude trades, overall availability stayed very comfortable. Due to these reasons, Hafnia's TCE decreased from $39,244/day to $24,452/day last quarter. Here's a detailed table for TCE per vessel class as well as for the fleet average.

Class

Q2-24 TCE/day

Q2-25 TCE/day

LR2

$60,116

$38,241

LR1

$46,986

$28,164

MR

$35,913

$22,967

Handy

$33,358

$19,808

Fleet avg

$39,244

$24,452

Source: Hafnia Filings, Author

Q3: Still Soft, But High-Yield Potential Remains

In Q3, spot rates remained weak. In late September, LR2 TC1 (MEG–Japan) slipped to WS ~112, TCE ~$22k/day, before bouncing back to WS ~119 in early October. MRs in the US Gulf were healthier (TC14 up from the WS170s to WS ~216, TCE ~$31k/day), while TC2 out of Europe fell at sub-$10k/day TCEs.

Hafnia itself had noted that 75% of Q3 days were covered at ~$25,395/day by mid-August, slightly higher than Q2’s realized TCE. There were also fewer off-hire days in Q3 (510 vs. 630). So while that better coverage may cushion the early-quarter rates lag, given how September actually traded, Q3 should be, give or take, very close to last quarter.

So, assuming that Q3 coverage is close to $25,400/day, even considering the uncovered days fixed around the September run-rate, my napkin math puts Q3 EPS around $0.14 to $0.16, matching last quarter's $0.15. At the same 80% payout, that implies again a roughly $0.12/share dividend, yielding ~8% dividend at today's share price. So even in such a challenging environment for the company, the stock's income potential remains very strong.

The TORM Deal: A New Income Stream

Earlier in September, Hafnia agreed to buy 14.45% of TORM (TRMD) from Oaktree at $22/share for $311.4 million. At the end of Q2, Hafnia had $194 million in cash on hand, and with its LTV at very healthy levels, I think this is a productive use of its cash. TRMD's dividend trend also follows that of HAFN, and the stock also yields around 8% today.

Instead of earning a much thinner interest rate on its cash, I believe the stake in TORM makes for a nice levered bet on the space while rates are low, as in a higher TCE environment, TORM's dividends will also rebound strongly and potentially yield anything from 10% to 30% based on how high rates go, i.e. what we saw in both HAFN and TORM last year.

And despite the levered nature of the bet (given the correlation to Hafnia), it seems that there is a wide margin of safety. TORM’s NAV was $23.5/share in Q2, so Hafnia appears to have bought below NAV. We must also assume that Hafnia's management has the expertise to determine if the stake in TRMD is worth it. They operate in the same space, and I like the way Hafnia has historically traded vessels, so I would choose to trust the investment in that regard.

Valuation and Risks

Hafnia marked its NAV at roughly $6.55/share at the end of Q2. It's different than the GAAP book value/share of $4.62, as its vessels are actually worth more in today's market. At today's share price of $5.77, this implies a discount of about 12%.

It's not around the way wider discounts of 30%-70% we often see in shipping, but that makes sense given the investor-friendly dividend policy and strong track record of execution. And again, considering that a strong rebound in rates could easily lead to Hafnia doubling its dividends from here (and still be below last year's dividend levels), the potential for dividend yields in the mid to high teens (and potentially higher) at the cost of today's share price is not that unlikely.

Of course, there are risks to holding Hafnia, with most related to how rates evolve from here. Rates may stay at underwhelming levels for quite some time, and the 8% yield, while notable, may not be large enough to keep investors interested (the space usually requires higher return potential given its cyclical nature, etc), which could widen the discount to NAV. That doesn't take away the future potential of rates and EPS/DPS bounding, but it could still lead to investors selling off the stock in the short term.

This is not unlikely given that the product tanker order book sits near 20% of the fleet, so even if the demand side improves materially, Hafnia and the rest of its peers might not be able to realize higher rates as the new ships coming online absorb much of that incremental demand. That could also be the case for TRMD, and that could affect Hafnia's balance sheet, as a decline in TRMD's price would boost leverage, thus potentially affecting Hafnia's dividend policy as well.

Therefore, while I believe Hafnia is attractively priced here and its dividends could really grow if tanker rates improve, today's setup makes for a levered bet on the space, especially following the investment in TORM, which warrants caution.

data.title: Molina Healthcare: Step Up In Medical Costs Creates An Attractive Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Summary

Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) is currently being heavily penalized by a perceived reduction in profitability due to higher utilization costs in the managed healthcare industry. We believe that new rates for Medicaid and Medicare will improve profitability, easing the negative pressure on Molina's stock price. Additionally, we are confident that the Most Favoured Nation policy pushed by Trump's administration will lead to a reduction in the cost of treatment, which will temporarily boost Molina's profitability. We believe Molina's fair price to be around $235, and we are issuing a BUY rating with a holding period of six to nine months.

Company Overview

Molina Healthcare provides managed healthcare services under the Medicaid and Medicare programs, and through the state insurance marketplace. The company has three business segments: Medicaid, Medicare, and Marketplace.

Medicaid represents by far the largest share of revenues, representing 79% of total revenues and 71% of profitability. Medicaid is the United States federal healthcare program for low-income individuals. It is funded by the federal government, but state-operated, resulting in 56 separate Medicaid programs. Under Medicaid, states pay healthcare providers a per-member per-month fee (that varies by state, demographic, and health risk factors), and in exchange medical care is provided to Medicaid members. States update their per-month per-member rates once a year, based on the current actuarial environment and under the supervision of the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services.

Medicare is the second largest segment, representing around 14.3% of both revenues and profitability. Medicare operates in a similar way to Medicaid, with the difference that Medicare covers the elderly and disabled beneficiaries, and that the CMS directly sets the rates instead of the states.

Profitability

The most important profitability metric for healthcare providers under public healthcare programs such as Medicaid and Medicare is the Health Benefits Ratio (HBR), defined (simplified) as medical costs divided by premium revenue. The higher the ratio (or equivalently, the higher the medical costs), the worse it is for the healthcare provider, as profitability worsens.

During 2024, the entire healthcare plan providers’ industry has been under severe pressure due to an overutilization of medical treatment, which has led to higher medical costs and driven profitability down. In H2 2024, rates have been increased to compensate for the overutilization during 2024, but the upwards trend in utilization continued at higher-than-expected levels, dragging the negative macroeconomic environment into 2025. We believe that, due to the persistent overutilization trend, new rates will be significantly higher, relieving negative pressure on profitability.

Moreover, we believe Molina is particularly well-positioned to benefit from this positive rate adjustment. Rates are typically set at the start of each state’s fiscal year, so mostly on July 1st (the fiscal year start date for 46 out of 50 states in the US). Then, proposed rates must be approved by the CSM, so they become “effective” for premiums renewed in late autumn. In Molina’s case, 55% of premiums are renewed on January 1st, meaning they will benefit early from new rates, improving profitability in Q1 26 (assuming utilization does not increase more than expected).

Despite threats to profitability, Molina provides a niche balance between size and stability, being among the 5 largest Medicaid managed care providers with 5 million members, retaining sufficient profitability (Medicaid-specific HBR ratio of 91.3%, better than Centene’s 94.9% and slightly more speculative than bigger companies, like UnitedHealth Group with an HBR of 89.4%). It has the resources to sustain the temporary increased healthcare utilization, and the stock price has been pushed down enough to make an investment in Molina highly attractive.

Negative momentum driven by utilization concerns

Molina's stock has lost more than 30% since last summer due to a series of negative news releases coming both from Molina and from industry peers such as Centene and Elevance Health.

In particular:

  • July 1st: Centene Corporation withdrew guidance due to higher medical costs, with a higher HBR. Molina dropped 22%
  • July 7th: Molina cuts guidance on earnings (from $24.5 to $22.33), citing “market dynamics and disclosure from others in the managed care sector”. Stock drops by 2.9%, as most of the cut was already priced in.
  • July 17th: Elevance Health cuts EPS guidance, due to higher Medicaid costs. Molina drops another 10%
  • July 23rd (after close): Molina reports Q2 earnings cutting guidance again (from $22.33 to $19), due to “challenging medical cost environment”, originated by “a temporary dislocation between premium rates and medical costs"

Despite the negative market reaction, we believe there are positive tailwinds coming in the near future.

Most Favoured Nation to drive medical costs lower

States set their annual rates through an actuarial process, which takes into account past utilization trends and the cost of treatment. This leaves companies like Molina exposed to an increase in drug prices and inflation, as could be seen by the weak performance in the last couple of years. However, we believe there could be a reduction in medical costs thanks to Trump's administration and its most favored nation policy.

On September 30th, Pfizer announced a deal with the government to provide medicines for Medicaid at the most favored nation price, and Pfizer received in return a three-year exemption from tariffs.

On October 10th, AstraZeneca followed closely, signing basically the same agreement, reducing drug prices for Medicaid, and receiving a three-year delay in tariffs.

We are convinced that rates set by the states will not be reactive to this reduction in price, as the guidelines provided by the CMS were issued in August, before these announcements. For this reason, we believe there will be a reduction in the cost of treatment while retaining an increased per-month per-member rate, which should reduce the HBR ratio for the managed healthcare industry.

Valuation

Due to the recent stock decline, Molina now trades at severely depressed multiples, with NTM EV/Revenues sitting at 0.21x, half of its 3-year average of 0.42x, and profitability multiples such as NTM P/E showing the company is being overly penalized by a temporary decline in profitability, with a 30% multiple compression from the three-year average NTM P/E multiple of 13.7x.

In a price-taker industry where the interests of the government, of healthcare providers, and of the public collide, we believe there must be a stable long-term equilibrium that satisfies all parties. That is exactly the logic behind rate adjustments, making any deviation from that long-term equilibrium temporary by definition.

For this reason, we believe that a fair multiple range for Molina, accounting for rate uncertainty, would be around 0.3x NTM EV/Revenue, 7.2x NTM EV/EBITDA, and 12x NTM P/E. All three of these multiples are significantly lower than Molina's three-year average NTM multiples, to reflect market pessimism on overutilization trends. The current market consensus expects Revenues in the next twelve months to be close to $45.7bn, a 10.9% increase YoY, with an EBITDA of $1.53bn (a 14.5% decline YoY) and an EPS of $17.9, down 11.6% from the actual EPS in the last twelve months.

We believe that the market consensus for EBITDA and EPS is overly conservative, as we believe there will be a positive impact from a lower cost of treatment, thanks to the most favored nation policy. Nevertheless, even basing our valuation of Molina on the current market consensus, according to our fair multiple range, the fair price for Molina would be around $235 per share.

Investment Risks

Our thesis mainly consists of two points: a favorable Medicaid rate adjustment given the higher past utilization trends, and a lower future cost of treatment due to the most favored nation policy. We believe both of these scenarios to be likely, and hence we believe Molina to be an attractive opportunity with a medium timeframe.

However, there is a risk that new Medicaid rates might be considered too low by the market, which is currently worried by budget constraints spread among many U.S. states. If new rates are disappointing, the weakness in the managed healthcare industry could continue for an additional year.

The most favored nation policy could drive medical costs down, which temporarily benefits Molina. However, next year the states will take note of the reduced costs and will reduce the rates, compressing revenues and negatively impacting the company's profitability due to fixed costs.

Conclusion

We believe Molina Healthcare offers an attractive entry opportunity for investors looking to hold the position for 6/9 months. In our view, the negative pressure on profitability is short-lived, while the downward pressure on the price is overdone. For these reasons, we are issuing a BUY rating on Molina, with a target price of $235.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - Tesla, Netflix Earnings, U.S. CPI And China's 5-Year Plan In Focus As U.S.-China Tensions Simmer
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review

Another week has passed by, and the US government shutdown continues. Despite this, the week was still full of volatility as markets grappled with the ongoing US-China stalemate as well as concerns around the US banking sector later in the week.

As a result, safe havens continued to thrive, with gold prices soaring to near $4400/oz before falling around 2.7% on Friday. The drop in gold came about as President Trump provided a sliver of hope on US-China relations, saying "not sustainable" and that he would meet with Xi Jinping in South Korea in a few weeks.

US equities enjoyed a mixed week and struggled on Thursday, weighed down by banking stocks. US banks borrowed nearly $15 billion from the Federal Reserve's Standing Repo Facility (SRF) on Wednesday and Thursday, the largest borrowing over a two-day period since the Covid-19 pandemic. Market participants were concerned about US credit markets as a result and whether that could affect valuations across markets.

The selloff on Thursday rippled through Asia overnight and weighed on European stocks on Friday, wiping out weekly gains. These concerns came about after major US banks reported stellar earnings earlier in the week.

Despite the mixed week for US and global equity markets, equity funds attracted inflows for a fourth straight week through October 15, as dovish comments from U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell reinforced expectations that the central bank will cut interest rates at its meeting later this month.

Across the globe, investors bought about $2.17 billion worth of stock funds this past week, a figure similar to the week before. This money mainly flowed into U.S. and Asian stock funds, which saw nearly $1 billion in inflows each.

Conversely, European stock funds saw a significant outflow of $1.62 billion, ending a ten-week period of continuous buying. Within the market, interest in funds focused on specific sectors surged by nearly 50%, with the technology and healthcare sectors leading all investments.

Next week the US reporting season heats up and could set the tone for global equity markets. After major banks reported this past week, next week will feature results from several major household names, including Tesla (TSLA) and Netflix (NFLX) which are among the most closely watched reports. We will also get reports from other big names like Procter & Gamble (PG), Coca-Cola (KO) and aerospace giant RTX (RTX) and tech veteran IBM (IBM).

How has the US Dollar Performed?

The U.S. dollar is finishing the week with losses against major global currencies. The overall dollar index, which tracks the US currency's value, is on track for a 0.44% weekly slide, its largest drop since late July despite a small gain of 0.26% on Friday.

The dollar weakened against the safe-haven Swiss franc, falling 0.1% to its lowest level since mid-September. Meanwhile, the euro was set for its best weekly gain against the dollar in nine weeks, even though it dipped slightly by 0.22% on Friday.

Finally, the dollar was flat against the Japanese yen, which is also on track to notch a weekly gain, and the British pound (sterling) was down slightly by 0.2% but still poised for a weekly gain.

This week also saw rate cut expectations rise, with market participants now pricing in around 51 bps of rate cuts through December 2025, up from around 44 bps earlier in the week. This is based on the LSEG data.

The Week Ahead

Next week will definitely be a busier one, as we did have a bit of a data break this week. Obviously the US Government shutdown has hampered US data releases, but there was also a lack of high impact data from around the world.

The week ahead brings a host of high impact data releases from around the globe, including inflation data from the US, China and Canada. While we also have a host of central bank policymakers speaking.

Let us take a look at some of the key data releases that could shake markets next week.

Asia Pacific Markets

China is facing a pivotal week that will set its economic direction for the next five years, even as new data is expected to confirm a recent slowdown.

From Monday to Wednesday, the Fourth Plenum meetings will be held, with the main goal of discussing China's important 15th Five-Year Plan for the years 2026 to 2030.

Key priorities that are expected to be highlighted include boosting consumer spending, driving technological innovation (especially in areas like AI and semiconductors to achieve self-reliance), and generally shifting toward "high-quality development" to secure long-term growth.

Separately, critical economic data is due on Monday:

Loan Prime Rates: The central bank is expected to keep these key interest rates unchanged.

GDP and Property: Official data is likely to show China's third-quarter economic growth slowed substantially to around 4.5% for the year. Key monthly data on retail sales and factory output are also expected to show a deceleration. Additionally, data on property prices is expected to confirm the market is still weak, as the government has not yet announced any major new stimulus to turn the sector around.

With the Bank of Japan's interest rate decision coming up on October 30th, two key pieces of economic data are highly important this week: trade and inflation figures.

Japan's exports are expected to rebound and grow by 4.0% compared to a year ago, mainly because shipments of goods like cars and chip-making machinery are returning to normal following the recent 15% tariff deal with the US.

At the same time, imports are likely to decrease slightly by 0.5%, largely because global commodity prices are lower. Analysts expect exports to continue normalizing in the coming months due to the September trade agreement.

The overall inflation rate is expected to rise to 2.9% for September, with core prices likely remaining above 3.0%. The main reason inflation has slowed down recently is due to temporary factors, specifically government subsidies for energy and social welfare programs.

Tariffs, Tariffs and More Tariffs

Due to the ongoing US government shutdown, there is a serious lack of clear information on how the economy is actually performing. The government's statistical agencies are closed, meaning key reports are delayed, and even when the government reopens, it will take weeks to properly collect and process the missing data.

However, one important piece of information, the September inflation report (CPI) has been confirmed for release. This is not for the Federal Reserve's benefit (even though they need it for their October 29th meeting), but because the number is legally required to calculate the Social Security cost-of-living increase for 2026.

Experts still expect the report to show a small rise in overall prices (0.4% for the month) and a modest increase in core prices (0.3%). Even if tariffs start making inflation more obvious, the Fed's biggest concern right now is the weakening job market, meaning a small rise in inflation will not stop them from making a planned quarter-point interest rate cut later this month.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index

This week's Chart of the week is the US Dollar Index (DXY). From a technical perspective, the DXY has broken a three-day losing streak, finding support at the 100-day MA on Friday.

The daily candle closed as a hammer candle, setting up the potential for further upside on Monday. If this level holds on Monday, the DXY could continue its rise in the early part of next week. This will also depend on US-China developments.

CPI data will be due on Friday, though, and market participants may be slightly hesitant to commit to any major moves ahead of the data release. This could potentially lead to some choppy price action in the early part of the week, something to consider.

Immediate resistance rests at 99.57 before the psychological 100.00 handle comes into focus. Looking at support and a break below the 100-day MA could lead to a retest of the 97.70 or 96.90 support levels.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart - October 17, 2025

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Volkswagen: Catalysts, Risks, And A Valuation That's Hard To Ignore
data.description:
Introduction

Since I first covered Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF), the stock is down about 7%, despite initially recording a ~12.5% increase.

This comes as a result of several macro and micro developments that can shape the company’s near-term (at least), so let’s take another look and update the thesis.

Internal Developments

VW has recently issued a profit warning as a result of Porsche (OTCPK:DRPRY)’s decision to slow its EV transition and delay the rollout of its all-electric SUV, citing weak demand, significant Chinese competition, and US tariffs, which we’ll talk about below.

As a result, we see an updated outlook that includes relatively flat sales revenue, a significant drop to 2% to 3% in operating profit margin and flat net cash flow, although their automotive net liquidity would still improve vs 2024. Still, when considering the €3 billion goodwill impairment related to Porsche, due to the lower mid-term ambitions (as well as the €2.1 billion cost on group level) and tariffs, this is not such a bad performance for a very discretionary business.

Still, due to the weak environment that’s been pressuring them lately, VW also announced that it would idle two factories in Germany back in late September. There's also a broader cost-saving program and plans to improve their margins and revenue, although tariffs took them by surprise and disrupted the industry significantly.

VW has also announced $1.2 billion worth of investments in AI-supported development, industrial applications, and the expansion of high-performance IT infrastructure, which it expects to deliver up to €4 billion in savings by 2035, advancing their cost savings efforts.

VW’s earnings are scheduled for October 30, with revenue expected to drop to $89.72 billion and GAAP EPS of $1.03. I’m looking forward to their insights into the economy, as well as potential investment plans given the recent discussions and competitive moves mentioned below.

Macro Developments

A major event even for VW, despite the US market not being that significant, is the September rate cuts and expectations of lower rates in the future. These are ultimately a tool to stimulate back the economy after being raised to fight inflation and slow down economic activity, increasing discretionary budgets by lowering the cost of debt. As auto is a very discretionary industry and one of the worst performers in a recession/slowdown (as we can see with virtually all players in recent years), continued global rate cuts will ultimately be a significant catalyst for VW - on top of lowering their own cost of debt and benefitting their cash flows directly.

Despite initial news a few months ago about European automakers being shielded to some degree following news that tariffs on car imports from the EU would be reduced from 27.5% to 15% back in late July, the situation around tariffs remains very uncertain. Even if we get to a resolution today (such as the $750 billion LNG deal with the EU), politics nowadays are so unpredictable that we can’t count on them not turning 180 degrees tomorrow. This happened a few days ago with China following Trump’s threats of adding extra 100% tariffs and now China being ready to retaliate and reignite the market’s fear of a trade war.

As auto is a very economy-dependent industry, even if it was completely exempt from any tariff/duty/etc., it would feel the significant effects of a weaker economy. I believe tariffs aren’t the “end goal” here, and they’re more of a tool to gain leverage in negotiations, but the longer they hurt the economy, the worse it is for companies like VW. What’s even worse is that these effects won’t be instant, and we may be looking at months or entire quarters of bad data ahead as a result, so volatility should be expected - even more than usual.

As a reminder, gold reached $4,200 per ounce recently (after breaking several records this year) for a very good reason, and that says something very clear about the market’s expectations around the US economy, the dollar, and global markets. Especially when paired with rate cuts, this can put the economy in a very tricky position and lead to a hard landing/recession/etc., which is a terrible environment for the auto industry.

However, although gold’s rise is more of a “figurative” impact rather than an actual one, remember that gold is not the only precious metal that’s booming this year. Platinum, which is used predominantly in catalytic converters for traditional cars to limit their emissions - which will be required more and more to meet newer pollution standards - is also having probably the best year in its history. Although it’s not the all-time record (that was back in March 2008, by the way, which might be a scary indicator), that still means higher costs for auto producers going forward, hurting their margins in a time when that’s the last thing they needed.

And all these issues are on top of the very intense competition coming from China when it comes to EVs. Although Europe has imposed some tariffs on that and the US did it even before Trump, Chinese EVs are a major disruptor to the company’s long-term in the segment, especially if we’re talking about other major markets than the EU/US/Australia. There’s not much they can do other than “join them” in producing cheaper EVs, although it’s realistically impossible to defeat Chinese players that are supported by state subsidies, which is why state support is still needed in other regions.

Also, one of their main competitors, Stellantis (STLA), has recently announced a nice recovery in shipments (up 13% YoY in Q3) following their inventory management actions in recent quarters while also announcing that it plans to invest $13 billion over the next 4 years in an effort to support its US manufacturing capabilities - the largest in the company’s history. Similarly, VW also had some “very positive” talks with Washington regarding increasing their US investments to offset tariffs back in September, which could give the market some more confidence when announced.

Reiterated Valuation

Again, for this valuation, I believe splitting Volkswagen Auto and Financial Services is very important, since FS has significant debt due to the way the business works.

For Volkswagen Auto, let's start with last year's free cash flow of €4 billion, which seems like a fair assumption before the unknown tariff impact. Considering the substantial investments in electrification and software (e.g. Rivian partnership, XPENG, CARIAD, etc.), better focus on China, lower CAPEX, cost savings, I'll project this FCF to grow at a CAGR of 6% for the initial 5 years. I know it might seem like a lot as they expect lower FCF this year, but we're talking about a long-term estimate that goes beyond just the currently bad environment, and getting to ~€5.57 billion by 2030 doesn't seem impossible (they're already adding €3 billion this year from CAPEX cuts announced before).

This will be followed by a more moderate 4% growth for the subsequent 5 years, and a terminal growth rate of 2%. Using a WACC of 7.5% (assumptions: ~9.6% cost of equity, ~4% cost of debt), the resulting enterprise value from discounting these automotive-focused cash flows is ~€89.32 billion. After subtracting the automobile net debt of €45 billion and then dividing by the total 501.3 million shares outstanding, the calculated equity value comes out to ~€44.32 billion, resulting in an intrinsic value per share of ~€88.41 (equivalent of $103.09), so nearly identical to the current price.

However, that's only the valuation for Volkswagen's Automobile division. On top of that, they have the Financial Services, and if we add it with a simple PB of 0.5 to the total, we're getting another €22.75 billion. That's a total €67.07 equity value, meaning a price of €133.79 (about $155.95), which is over a 50% profit from the current level. Again, we're starting with a conservative estimated average cash flow, but in reality, it can be significantly better or worse, depending on the environment.

Conclusions

I reiterate Volkswagen as a Buy given the attractive valuation and turnaround potential in a very beaten-down industry today, with the overall risk-reward starting to look quite appealing. Of course, this depends significantly on the economy, so it remains a bet on whether you believe that will improve or not, and there’s definitely potential to see it get worse before it gets any better, so it’s ultimately a matter of the margin of safety you’re happy with.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLV: Lower Interest Rates And A Positive Growth Outlook Will Pave The Way
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV) a buy rating. I am of the opinion that the healthcare industry will experience significant growth in the upcoming years. Since August 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve System (Fed) has begun to cut interest rates, which will contribute to the allocation of new funds into the biopharma industry. Secondly, the legislation implemented by U.S. President Donald Trump, which mandates drugmakers to lower the selling price of their medicine, has not been severe in its implementation. Recently, in an agreement between Pfizer and the U.S. government, Pfizer agreed to sell some of its medicines at a discount to only Medicaid beneficiaries. As such, this legislation will not be extended to all Americans. These factors have held back XLV's price performance.

XLV is an ETF with a low expense ratio, and it has the highest assets under management (AUM) among comparable healthcare ETFs. Additionally, the top ten holdings of XLV are companies with excellent profit margins and a positive growth outlook. In fact, the median expected revenue growth of these companies is 6.53%.

However, there are several risks that could undermine the performance of XLV. The U.S. may continue to implement more regulations that could negatively affect the companies that constitute XLV. For instance, it could continue to reduce the budget allocated to the U.S. healthcare public system. In addition, higher tariffs on imported goods to the U.S. could also impact XLV. Despite all these risks, I think that the positive aspects of XLV outweigh the negative aspects. Therefore, I consider XLV to be an excellent investment opportunity.

Motivation

This is my second article on XLV, an ETF that could experience a significant increase in its share price in the upcoming years. As depicted in the following chart, XLV tracked the S&P 500 Index (SP500) up to December 2022. However, since that month, the SP500 experienced a significant gain, while the XLV has remained relatively flat. I think that the gap between the SP500 performance and the XLV performance will narrow. It is difficult to pinpoint when that will happen.

I consider that there are three primary factors that explain the gap between XLV and SP500: COVID-19, high interest rates, and U.S. legislation toward the healthcare industry. Albeit, I think that these factors will not continue to hold back the share price of XLV.

Interest Rates

High interest rates have an adverse effect on any industry. When interest rates are high, the cost of borrowing money increases. The starting point of this analysis begins with COVID-19. With the lowdown, countries lower the interest rates to stimulate the economy. Otherwise, the lockdown would have a more devastating effect on the economy.

From April 2020 to February 2022, the Federal Funds Effective Rate (FFER) was equal to or below 0.1%, as depicted in the following chart. This is the interest rate that the Fed uses to stimulate the American economy. It also has an effect on the U.S. Treasury yields. The Fed lowered the interest rate to mitigate the negative effects of the pandemic on the U.S.

From February 2022 to August 2023, the FFER experienced a significant increase, reaching 5.33% at the end of the previous time interval. When COVID-19 was no longer a problem for society, the Fed began to increase the FFER, concluding its policy of low interest rates. However, there was too much stimulus in the economy, and the Fed increased the interest rate to values not seen in more than 15 years.

However, the Fed began to cut the FFER. Since August 2024, this rate has declined. That month, it was 5.33, decreasing to 4.22 in September of this year. For the rest of the year, the Fed expects to implement more cuts to the FFER.

The following chart shows how the interest rate affects the biopharma industry. Since 2021, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield has experienced a significant increase. During the same period, venture capital deployment has declined in the biopharma industry, which has affected XLV's performance.

High interest rates have disincentivized the deployment of capital in the biopharma industry. As such, there is less funding flowing into this market. However, if the interest rates continue to decline, it will incentivize more credits into the biopharma industry.

The following table shows the debt-to-equity ratio and net income margin of Eli Lilly (LLY), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), AbbVie (ABBV), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Merck & Co. (MRK), Thermo Fisher (TMO), Amgen (AMGN), Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), and Pfizer (PFE). These are the top ten holdings of XLV, accounting for 57.0% of XLV's AUM. These companies depend on debt to finance their operations. In fact, the median debt-to-equity ratio is 69.70%. Therefore, if the Fed reduces the interest rates, it will have a positive effect on the cost structure of these companies.

U.S. Legislation

The U.S. government has implemented several pieces of legislation to reduce its expenses allocated to the public healthcare industry. In my previous article, I highlighted some of the cuts that the government expects to implement. For a brief review of those cuts, I suggest the reader check that article.

In my previous article, I indicated that the government plans to cut the budget allocated to Medicaid, Medicare, and the Affordable Care Act by $930 billion in the next ten years. I also wrote that the U.S. total expenditure on health was $4,900 billion in 2023. As such, the expected cut represents 18.98% of the U.S. health expenditure of 2023. However, I did not emphasize that this cut will be implemented in ten years. If I divide 18.98% by ten, the result is approximately 1.90%. This last value is the percentage at which the aforementioned cuts will affect the U.S. health expenditure every year.

Secondly, many of the companies that compose XLV have global operations. For instance, during 2024, 41.56% of ABT's sales came from outside of the U.S. This company is among the top ten holdings of XLV. As such, the U.S. legislation will have a partial effect on XLV due to its global exposure.

The U.S. President Donald Trump sent a letter to the top 17 drugmakers in the U.S. In this letter, he instructed these companies to lower the selling price of their medicines. However, so far, the government has not implemented drastic measures against American drugmakers.

As a consequence of the letter sent by the U.S. president, Pfizer reached an agreement with the American government, in which it will sell some of its medicines at a significant discount. However, this agreement will only include the medicine sold to the Medicaid program. Therefore, I think the discount that drugmakers will have to implement on the selling price of their medicine will not have a substantial negative impact on their revenues. As depicted in the following chart, XLV positively reacted to the agreement between Pfizer and the U.S. government, which the company announced on September 30, 2025.

Advantage

The life expectancy has increased in the U.S. and around the world. I discussed how life expectancy has increased worldwide and in the U.S. in my previous XLV write-up. Better medical treatments have contributed to an increase in life expectancy. As depicted in the following image, the number of people who die due to cancer, who are 50 years or younger, has declined worldwide. Cancer is merely one example that explains how medical treatments have contributed to increasing life expectancy. The critical point is that, since people live longer, they require more medicines to increase their life expectancy, especially patients with chronic diseases.

Companies that comprise XLV benefit from this factor. For instance, if a patient dies at 30 years due to cancer, he will no longer buy more medicines. However, if he is successfully treated and cured, he will likely continue to buy new medicines. As such, the biopharma industry will continue to generate profit from that person.

Clinical Trials

Companies and institutions have continued to develop more medicines. Before introducing a new medical treatment to the market, it is mandatory to test the efficiency and safety of that treatment. If there are more studies, it is a sign that there will be more medicine in the years to come. As observed in the following chart, the number of medical studies with posted results has consistently increased. These results are from ClinicalTrials.gov, an online database where companies and institutions post their clinical research studies. Returning to the main point, the chart reveals that companies and institutions continue to develop new medical treatments. As such, there is no sign that companies have reduced investment in developing new medicines. The results of 5,846 studies have been posted this year as of October 08.

Top Holdings

Investing in XLV will expose investors to the top healthcare companies in the U.S. The following table shows the top ten holdings of XLV, which are LLY, JNJ, ABBV, UNH, ABT, MRK, TMO, AMGN, ISRG, and PFE.

For this year, the top ten holdings of XLV expect a median revenue growth of 6.53%. Meanwhile, the median expected growth of the EBITDA is 8.70%. These figures suggest that these companies will not encounter a difficult financial situation.

Another positive aspect is that these companies are profitable. As depicted in the table below, they have significant profit margins. Excluding UNH, which is a healthcare provider (healthcare providers tend to have a lower profit margin), the remaining companies have EBITDA margins above 25%. In addition, these companies have a median levered FCF margin of 16.84%. The last figure indicates that these firms generate significant financial resources to fund their operations and return dividends to their shareholders.

The key point is that the top ten holdings of XLV do not show signs of financial distress. By investing in XLV, more than half of the portfolio will comprise companies with strong financial performance.

Peers

So far, this article has discussed the benefits of investing in the healthcare sector. However, it has not compared XLV with other healthcare ETFs. As such, this section implements a comparative analysis among XLV, Vanguard Health Care Index Fund ETF Shares (VHT), iShares Global Healthcare ETF (IXJ), iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (IYH), Fidelity MSCI Health Care Index ETF (FHLC), Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight Health Care ETF (RSPH), iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB), and SPDR® S&P Biotech ETF (XBI). The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

XLV has $36 billion in AUM, the highest figure among its peers, representing a positive aspect. ETFs with high AUM tend to have lower expense ratios. In fact, XLV has a 0.08% expense ratio, the lowest value among its peer group.

A low expense ratio is critical when choosing an ETF. The current expense ratio of XLV is 0.08%. For example, if XLV averages that expense ratio (every year) for the next ten years, investors will incur only 0.8% in fees during that period, which is less than 1%. To provide another example, IBB has the highest expense ratio, with 0.44%. If this percentage remains constant over the next ten years, investors will be required to pay 4.4% in fees to maintain their position. In the last ten years, IBB's price performance was 48.14%. Assuming that IBB's price performance will be 48.14% over the following ten years, and also assuming a total expense ratio of 4.4% for that period, the expense ratio will represent 9.14% of IBB's price performance. As such, fees will eat a significant portion of the potential return. By investing in XLV, investors will avoid this problem.

XLV has consecutively paid dividends for 15 years, which is a positive aspect. Meanwhile, the other ETFs in the peer group have consecutively paid dividends for lower periods of time. In my opinion, this aspect is a positive sign. It transmits confidence to investors. Further, XLV paid a dividend yield of 1.69% in the last year, the highest value in the peer group. Additionally, over the last four years, XLV paid an average dividend yield of 1.55%, while the median ratio was 1.23%. Investors who have held XLV over the last four years have earned an additional 0.32% in dividend yields compared to the median ratio. This value may seem small, but over a ten-year period, it makes a significant difference.

I think that the price performance of a financial instrument is the most critical metric to evaluate, since an investor will obtain most of its profit from the ETF's price performance. The table below shows the ratios to analyze. In the last year, XLV's price performance was the worst among its peers. However, it achieved the highest return with 35.32% in the past five years. Furthermore, the 10-year price performance of XLV ranks fourth among its peers. Consequently, investing in XLV will provide investors with significant returns over a 5-to 10-year period.

Disadvantages

There is no perfect ETF, and some of the benefits that an ETF can provide are countered by negative aspects. As depicted in the following table, XLV is comprised of only 61 financial instruments, considerably lower than the median holding. In addition, the top ten holdings of XLV account for 57.07% of the portfolio, which is the highest figure in the peer group. If one of the securities in XLV goes bankrupt, it will have a higher impact on XLV compared to the other ETFs in the peer group. The other ETFs are composed of more financial instruments.

The tracking error is another negative aspect of XLV. During the last year and over a five-year period, XLV reported higher tracking errors than the median values in the peer group, as is evident in the table below. The reader might wonder how to interpret the tracking error. For example, imagine that XLV has a tracking error of 14.55% (its current 1-year tracking error), and also imagine that the benchmark that XLV tracks generates a 20% return this year. This indicates that XLV's return will fall within the range of 5.45% (20% - 14.55%) to 34.55% (20% + 14.55%), in about 68% of the time.

Risks

The U.S. legislation has implemented significant cuts to the budget of the U.S. public healthcare system. So far, the new U.S. administration has been in place for less than one year. It is hard to say what could occur in the next years, but among the possible scenarios, the government could implement more cuts to the U.S. public healthcare system.

The U.S. has shifted its trade policy, imposing higher tariffs on imported goods. The biopharma industry could be affected due to this dispute. Recently, the U.S. government imposed a 100% tariff on imported branded drugs that do not have plants under construction in the U.S. to produce those branded medicines. Countries can also implement the same policy for American biopharmaceutical products. If this scenario occurs, the share price of XLV will be negatively affected.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I think that XLV represents an excellent investment opportunity. There are several factors that have prevented XLV's price from reaching a higher level. The first one is the high interest rate, which has disincentivized investment in the biopharma industry. However, the Fed has begun to reduce the FFER, which will incentivize the inflow of money into this industry. Secondly, to date, the U.S. government has not implemented strict legislation in the healthcare industry in the United States. For instance, the president sent a letter to 17 leading drugmaker companies in the U.S., in which he stated that these firms must lower the selling prices of their medicines. Recently, Pfizer, one of the 17 companies, agreed with the government to sell some of their medicines at discounts, but these discounts will only be applied to Medicaid beneficiaries. Therefore, it will affect a proportion of Pfizer's revenues.

XLV is an ETF with an expense ratio of only 0.08%. In addition, it has the highest AUM among several relevant healthcare ETFs. Some of the risks are that XLV is comprised of only 61 financial instruments, and its top ten holdings account for 57.07% of this ETF. Other healthcare ETFs are more diversified. Additionally, XLV has a high tracking error, which could affect its performance. Furthermore, the context of high tariffs may negatively affect the biopharma industry. Despite these risks, I still consider XLV to be an excellent investment opportunity.

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values At Lowest Level Since March 2021
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends. Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values rose for the first time in seven months in September, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values dropped for a 17th consecutive month, remaining at their lowest level since March 2021.

Last month's ZHVI came in at $363,932, up 0.07% from the previous month and up 0.10% from one year ago. However, after adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.31% month-over-month and -3.76% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its “Zestimates,” its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P at CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is 0.69% off its all-time high from February 2025.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.79% off its all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes. This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI are "the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Home Price Index. Zillow claims this is due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction. Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are "substantially different from non-distressed sales" and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents "two very different market segments." Eric Stubbs, Ph.D., at RBC WM believes foreclosure sales is a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors

data.title: Bitcoin Is At Trend Support
data.description:
After what had been a truly terrific 500% rise since the start of 2023, I turned considerably more cautious on Bitcoin (BTC-USD) in July and downgraded the coin to a 'hold.' I reiterated the asset as a 'hold' through coverage of the ProShares Bitcoin Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:BITO) in August and again through the Franklin Bitcoin ETF (BATS:EZBC) in September - each time, taking a slightly different angle in my coverage.

After several months of chop, it appeared as though Bitcoin was ready for smooth sailing following a breakout to a nominal new all time high earlier in October. That breakout is proving to be a fake-out so far. As of article submission, Bitcoin is currently hanging on to support near $107k per coin after knifing through its 200 day moving average early in the October 17th session.

Recapping The Summer Downgrade

My primary concerns when I downgraded Bitcoin to a 'hold' in July could best be summed up into three main points:

  1. Lack of on-chain usage growth, "adoption" through ETF speculation
  2. Proliferation of DATs reminiscent of the 'Blockchain' mania in 2017
  3. Capital flows generally shifting to Ethereum (ETH-USD) over Bitcoin

As I see it, on-chain data continues to favor Ethereum over Bitcoin on several levels. First, the daily active user trend for Ethereum is positive, while Bitcoin peaked two years ago.

September was the second month where Ethereum bested Bitcoin by average DAAs - and this notably doesn't include L2 networks or scaling chains, where ETH has a significant advantage over BTC. Transactions favor ETH, and valuation does as well:

Despite Bitcoin retaking the lead in dollar-denominated transferred value from Ethereum in September, at current prices investors are paying more than a 3x premium for Bitcoin's network when measured by the 30-day average NVT ratio. From a capital flow standpoint, Bitcoin has had a significant resurgence against ETH in recent weeks:

Asset (mil) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin $5,128 $30,212 $188,694
Ethereum $1,071 $13,998 $36,456
Multi-asset -$51.6 -$49 $7,684
Solana $196.1 $2,670 $4,795
XRP -$50.9 $1,889 $2,941
Total* $6,343 $48,715 $242,503
BTC Dominance 80.8% 62.0% 77.8%
ETH Dominance 16.9% 28.7% 15.0%

Source: CoinShares, Bloomberg, through October 10th, *includes assets not shown in the table

So far through the first third of October, Bitcoin dominance in the capital flow game is better than 80%. At one point this summer, ETH dominance in YTD capital flows hit 34%; significant because ETH's real dominance ratio by market cap has been more range-bound between 12-15%. Still, at 28.7% year-to date flow dominance, Ethereum has been punching well above its weight this year. Again, Bitcoin is admittedly off to a better start in October. But when we look at fresher data from Farside, the reaction following the October 10th liquidation event has been to de-risk BTC to a larger degree:

$ in millions BTC ETF Flows ETH ETF Flows
13 Oct 2025 -$326.4 -$428.5
14 Oct 2025 $102.7 $236.2
15 Oct 2025 -$104.1 $169.6
16 Oct 2025 -$530.9 -$56.8
Weekly total -$858.7 -$79.5

Source: Farside Investors

Through Thursday October 16th, nearly $860 million has come out of Bitcoin investment products this week. It's less than $100 million for Ethereum. But the larger point is, as quickly as this liquidity can bid the price up, so too can it take the price down if investors shift to a risk-off sentiment. The other thing to consider is Bitcoin's whale buyer has all but stopped purchasing through October:

Through the first two weeks of October, Strategy's (MSTR)(STRF) Bitcoin acquisitions have totaled just $27 million. Although the company has an ability to raise capital for BTC purchased through its various preferred stock ATMs, more than 89% of the capital raised to buy BTC has come from the common stock ATM since company started selling preferred stocks in March. With basic mNAV compression progressively deteriorating from 2 to 1.18 since November, Strategy's easy road to BTC buys is running out of ground as every buy with preferred share capital creates a perpetual liability.

Open Interest And The Liquidation Event

I briefly mentioned the October 10th event in the prior section. During that liquidation, Bitcoin open interest declined from $90.2 billion to $70.5 billion in a single day. And in that major market deleveraging, Bitcoin saw a $20k high-to-low candle on numerous major exchanges, including Kraken, which actually experienced a $22k single-day drawdown.

While the coin had indeed bounced off lows and even tested $116k shortly thereafter, a major liquidation of this magnitude can certainly have an impact on BTC price further out. For the benefit of comparison, I'd like to show 1, 2, and 3-month returns for BTC following a few other single-day open interest meltdowns in what I'm calling the institutional post-COVID era:

Post-COVID Meltdowns May 19th 2021 September 7th 2021 December 4th 2021
BTC OI Decline $4.9b $4.4b $6.1b
BTC Price $37,341 $46,778 $48,204
1m Change -3.3% 15.4% -4.9%
2mo Change -17.5% 44.5% -14.2%
3mo Change 25.21% 10.48% -18.85%

Source: CoinGlass

To be clear, I'm tracking large declines in open interest, not single-day price declines. What this shows is the potential connection between significant declines in OI relative to how BTC performs shortly after. This table suggests that large declines in OI over a single day lead to mixed returns over the next several months. My assumption is the poor performance following the December 4th, 2021 OI decline is simply due to where Bitcoin was in its halving cycle.

Unless we're of the view that halving cycle theory is no longer relevant, I'd say Q4 2025 is about the time to be lightening up exposures. Given the liquidation even on October 10th, the nearly $20 billion single day decline in Bitcoin open interest, and our current position in the four year halving cycle, I suspect returns over the following few months will be more similar to the December 4th, 2021 returns than the other two examples.

Of course, there is a very real possibility that Bitcoin is simply testing long term trend support and will rebound from current levels. I'd be remiss if I did not mention that the 12 month MA has served as trendline support 3 times over the course of the last 15 months, and BTC just tagged that line again through Kraken. But the counter to that point would be that as each cycle progresses, Bitcoin builds more bearish RSI divergences. We can see this from the 2017 through 2025 cycles as well as from February 2024 to today.

Closing Takeaways

I want to be very clear; I still own Bitcoin. I'm still long BTC because I believe there remains a possibility that it can serve as a useful cross-border settlement asset. I do believe it will have a lot of competition there, with tokenized Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) on networks like Ethereum being a very formidable foe for Bitcoin's 'Digital Gold' narrative. Still, Bitcoin has something that no other digital asset has; first-mover advantage. And there's a lot of sticky value in that. However, none of this means I think Bitcoin is screaming 'buy' today. In fact, I'm becoming even more convinced that we've seen the top for this halving cycle already. From where I sit, the technicals aren't great, and the cycle is getting ripe. And purely speculating here, I suspect there is far more damage from the $19 billion liquidation event on October 10th than is currently understood. When Terra Luna collapsed in May 2022, it took 6 more months to figure out who all of the counterparty casualties were. The post-Luna period turned out to not be a good time to be long BTC, looking out the next 12-18 months. I have reduced my exposure to BTC through both the ETFs and the mining stocks.

data.title: Mammoth Energy Services: Increases Investments In Its Aviation Portfolio
data.description:
Mammoth Energy Services (NASDAQ:TUSK) has changed its business focus. It sold off some of its infrastructure subsidiaries (that accounted for around 85% to 90% of its infrastructure business) and its pressure pumping equipment for a combined total of $123.7 million, and has been investing in its aviation portfolio.

I believe that it received a good price for the sale of its infrastructure subsidiaries, but the sale of its pressure pumping equipment was underwhelming and also reduces Mammoth's upside in a recovery scenario due to its exit from the well completion services business.

Mammoth is a much smaller company now as a result of these sales and is attempting to reach breakeven free cash flow in the near-term.

Based on its projected cash on hand and its remaining businesses, I estimate Mammoth's value at $3.20 per share now. This is around $1 less than my prior estimate of Mammoth's value, as that higher valuation was largely dependent on a partial recovery in Mammoth's well completion services business.

Sale Of Pressure Pumping Equipment

Mammoth sold off its pressure pumping equipment for $15 million. This appears to be below book value, as Mammoth's pressure pumping (well completion services) division had $35 million in net property, plant, and equipment on its balance sheet at the end of 2024, which declined by $31 million to $4 million in net property, plant, and equipment at the end of Q2 2025 (post-divestiture).

Mammoth's well completion services division was once a major contributor to its results, generating $33.7 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2022 and $15.9 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2023. However, it reported negative $9.1 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2024 from that division.

Mammoth had previously been waiting for demand to pick back up, but it decided to exit that area with natural gas prices still around $3.

While the price it received for its equipment appears relatively low, much of its equipment was also getting outdated, and selling it removes the need for further spending on upgrades.

However, exiting this business also significantly reduces Mammoth's potential upside. Well completion services could have added $10 million to $20 million EBITDA to Mammoth's results in a recovery scenario.

Continuing Businesses And Investments

Mammoth has been investing in its aviation portfolio, spending $25 million year-to-date in that area. This includes the eight small passenger aircraft it purchased in April for around $11.5 million.

Mammoth is aiming for a 2x to 3x return on invested capital from its aviation investments over a three to five year time frame.

Mammoth expects negative $3 million to negative $4 million in adjusted EBITDA from its continuing operations in 2H 2025. This includes the impact of around $2 million to $2.5 million in legal fees (related to the Puerto Rico litigation).

Mammoth believes that declining legal fees after 2H 2025 plus aviation growth should then help it soon get to neutral free cash flow with its continuing operations.

Cash Position

Mammoth had $157 million in cash at the end of Q2 2025 (including $30 million in restricted cash). It also had $48 million in income taxes payable, so its cash position (net of income taxes payable) is $109 million.

Mammoth is owed $20 million from PREPA, payable within seven days of the effective date of PREPA's plan of adjustment with its bankruptcy proceedings.

Mammoth projects around $15 million in capex from continuing operations in 2H 2025. It also expects a $3 to $4 million EBITDA loss from continuing operations in the second half of the year and $4 million to $5 million in cash burn related to discontinued operations.

I am modeling Mammoth's year-end 2025 cash position at $89 million (net of income taxes payable). This is based on the above inputs plus several million in interest income generated by Mammoth's cash position.

The $89 million figure doesn't include the $20 million still due from PREPA, as that bankruptcy case continues to drag along. This also doesn't include any further proceeds from the sale of underutilized assets.

Notes On Valuation

Mammoth is projected to end 2025 with $89 million (or around $1.85 per share) in cash, net of income taxes payable. Adding the $20 million due from PREPA (and discounting that by 25% due to the uncertainty around payment timing) would bring this up to $104 million (or approximately $2.16 per share).

Adding $50 million in value for Mammoth's remaining businesses would bring its value up to $154 million (approximately $3.20 per share) at the end of 2025.

Mammoth has spent $25 million on its aviation portfolio so far this year, and much of its remaining $15 million capex budget is expected to be growth capex for its aviation portfolio and equipment rental business.

So given that it will have spent close to $40 million on acquiring assets during 2025, a valuation of $50 million for its continuing businesses may be conservative. However, Mammoth is expected to be at slightly negative to neutral adjusted EBITDA in the near-term.

Conclusion

Mammoth has divested much of its previous main businesses and is increasing its investments in its aviation portfolio now. It is projected to end 2025 with $89 million in cash net of income taxes payable now, and is also owed a further $20 million from PREPA.

This gives Mammoth a good financial position as it attempts to reach breakeven cash flow in the near-term. However, while the exit from the well completion services business reduces the variability of Mammoth's results, it also significantly reduces Mammoth's upside in a recovery scenario.

I now estimate Mammoth value at approximately $3.20 per share.
